items.q: Why is injection moulding suited to high-volume production?
items.correct: Tooling cost is high but per-unit cost is very low at scale.
items.misconception: It is the cheapest method for any quantity.
items.random_wrong: It is the only way to make plastic items.
items.why: Students think a 'good' process is good for everything. Injection moulding has expensive moulds - it's only economical when costs are spread over thousands or millions of parts.

items.q: What property do shape memory alloys have?
items.correct: They return to a pre-set shape when heated.
items.misconception: They remember every shape they have ever been bent into.
items.why: Students think SMAs are infinitely programmable. They actually have ONE 'remembered' shape, set during high-temperature treatment. Heating restores that shape; the alloy doesn't store a history of deformations.

items.q: What is FEA used for in product design?
items.correct: Simulating how a product will respond to forces, heat, or vibration before manufacture.
items.misconception: Final exam analysis of student designs.
items.random_wrong: Counting the elements in a chemical compound.
items.why: Students unfamiliar with the acronym guess at it. FEA = Finite Element Analysis: dividing a part into many small elements and simulating physical behaviour computationally - saves prototyping costs.

items.correct: A live virtual model synced by data
items.why: It's A live virtual model synced by data. “A static CAD model” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: The digital twin concept was articulated by Grieves around:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 2002. “1992” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: If a twin updates every 5 seconds, how many updates per hour?
items.why: The real figure is 720. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
